name: Hadolint Dockerfile Linter
what_it_does: Open-source Dockerfile linter and best practices enforcer — enabling developers and DevOps engineers to validate Dockerfiles against the official Docker best practices and CIS Docker Benchmark rules, integrating into CI/CD pipelines via CLI or shell operator, with a REST API provided by hadolint.io web service and shell script parsing via ShellCheck integration for RUN commands.
use_cases: Linting Dockerfiles in CI/CD pipelines via hadolint CLI for automated quality enforcement
Checking Dockerfile best practices via hadolint API for web-based linting tools
Integrating hadolint into editor plugins via Language Server Protocol (LSP) for real-time feedback
Enforcing security best practices in Dockerfiles via hadolint for container security
Validating shell commands in RUN instructions via ShellCheck integration in hadolint
not_for: Runtime container security (use Falco for runtime protection — hadolint is static)
Container vulnerability scanning for CVEs (use Trivy or Anchore for vulnerability DB scanning)
Kubernetes manifest validation (use kube-score or kube-linter for K8s YAML)
best_when: A development team wants to enforce Dockerfile best practices and security guidelines in CI/CD pipelines or editors — hadolint is the industry-standard Dockerfile linter.
avoid_when: You need runtime security, CVE scanning, or Kubernetes manifest validation — hadolint is Dockerfile-specific static analysis only.

auth.notes: No authentication required. Open-source tool — run locally via CLI (hadolint Dockerfile). Web API at hadolint.io: POST /v1/validate with Dockerfile content (no auth). Output formats: tty, json, checkstyle, codeclimate, gitlab_codeclimate, gnu, codacy, sarif. LSP server mode: hadolint --lsp for editor integration. Docker image available: hadolint/hadolint.

pricing.notes: Written in Haskell. MIT license. GitHub: hadolint/hadolint (10,000+ stars). Created by Lukas Martinelli. Maintained by community. ShellCheck integration: validates shell commands in RUN instructions. Rules based on: Docker official best practices, CIS Docker Benchmark, community contributions. Rule codes: DL (Dockerfile linting), SC (ShellCheck rules). Configurable via .hadolint.yaml. CI integrations: GitHub Actions, GitLab CI, Jenkins, CircleCI. SARIF output for GitHub Code Scanning integration. Widely adopted as the standard Dockerfile linter.

performance.rate_limits: No documented rate limits; self-hosted CLI has no limits

agent_readiness.error_message_notes: No auth required. CLI-first tool (hadolint Dockerfile). Web API: POST to hadolint.io/v1/validate. MIT open source. SARIF/JSON output. ShellCheck integration. Widely documented.
agent_readiness.idempotency_notes: Linting is deterministic — same Dockerfile always produces same results. Fully idempotent. No state stored.
known_agent_gotchas: Primary usage is CLI: hadolint Dockerfile — web API at hadolint.io is secondary
hadolint.io web API: POST Dockerfile content as request body to /v1/validate
Configuration: .hadolint.yaml or --ignore DL3008 to suppress specific rules
ShellCheck rules (SC prefix) can be noisy for complex shell — configure ignore list
Rule DL3008: no --no-install-recommends (apt), DL3009: delete apt-get lists after install
SARIF output format: --format sarif — useful for GitHub Code Scanning integration

security_score_components.security_notes: No credentials required — zero auth attack surface. MIT open source — auditable Haskell code. Dockerfile content sent to hadolint.io (use local CLI for sensitive Dockerfiles). No data persistence (stateless linting). Enforces security best practices (DL3 rules). ShellCheck catches shell injection patterns in RUN commands. User-controlled data residency via local CLI usage.
